region_of_origin: Teochew (Chaozhou), Guangdong, China; established as a Singapore hawker staple
food_subgroup: Rice porridge set — watery gruel with preserved and braised side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Teochew Muay as a prepared Singapore hawker porridge set. Closest parent would be a rice porridge dish concept; no exact URI available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice porridge (congee) with preserved side dishes — closest parent match
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0KDA is the closest defensible parent for rice porridge; no specific code exists for Teochew Muay set with preserved side dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Teochew Muay porridge set as a prepared hawker dish.

main_ingredients: watery rice gruel (fully gelatinised white rice)

serving.description: 1 set (~520g): watery rice gruel ~250ml + salted egg half ~30g + braised pork belly ~30g + braised peanuts ~20g + preserved radish/chai poh ~15g + pickled cucumber slices ~15g
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of Teochew Muay is approximately 520g, comprising a bowl of watery rice gruel (~250ml) accompanied by four side dishes: half a salted egg, a slice of braised pork belly (30g), braised peanuts (20g), and preserved radish/chai poh (15g) with pickled cucumber slices.

nutrient_source.title: Teochew Muay — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for individual components (watery rice gruel, salted egg, braised pork belly, braised peanuts, preserved radish, pickled cucumber) and aggregated to a per-100g basis for the full 520g set. Per-100g values represent the composite dish. Sodium is dominated by the salted egg half (~600mg) and chai poh (~225mg), yielding ~1,050mg per set and ~202mg per 100g of the composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice porridge / congee analogous estimate; watery rice gruel (fully gelatinised white rice starch)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~72 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice congee / watery rice gruel (fully gelatinised white rice). Watery rice porridge consistently shows GI in the range 69–78 due to complete starch gelatinisation and minimal retrograded resistant starch formation. GL per 520g set calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 38.1) / 100 ≈ 27.4. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — ~1,050mg per set (202mg per 100g), representing ~52.5% of the WHO 2,000mg daily sodium limit in a single meal, driven primarily by the salted egg half (~600mg Na) and preserved radish/chai poh (~225mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be counselled to substitute the salted egg with a fresh egg (saves ~560mg Na) and omit chai poh (saves ~225mg Na), which together can reduce the set's sodium load by ~785mg — bringing it within a more acceptable range for a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load (~27.4 per set) due to fully gelatinised watery rice gruel — the highest GI form of rice preparation, with minimal retrograded resistant starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should be advised that watery rice gruel carries a significantly higher glycaemic impact than firm-cooked rice (GI ~72 vs ~58–64); where possible, request firm-cooked rice instead of watery gruel, and note that the protein from pork belly, egg, and peanuts provides only partial GI moderation insufficient to offset the very-high-GI rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from very high sodium (~1,050mg per set), moderate saturated fat from pork belly and egg yolk (~7.8g per set), and elevated cholesterol (~338mg per set from salted egg yolk and pork belly)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: CVD patients should limit Teochew Muay consumption or substantially modify the set by replacing the salted egg with a fresh egg, omitting chai poh, and reducing pork belly portion, as the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol presents a compounded cardiovascular risk in a single meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium (~1,050mg per set) is the primary RA concern due to cardiovascular comorbidity management; pork belly contributes saturated fat which may modestly promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients managing cardiovascular comorbidities should apply the same sodium-reduction modifications as for hypertension; the traditional preservation process for salted egg and chai poh does not provide specific RA-relevant anti-inflammatory nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teochew Muay (潮州糜, Cháozhōu Mí) is a traditional Teochew Chinese rice porridge set that has become a beloved Singapore hawker staple. The dish centres on a bowl of very watery, fully gelatinised white rice gruel (~250ml) — thinner and more liquid than Cantonese congee — served alongside four preserved and braised side dishes: half a salted egg, a slice of braised pork belly (30g), braised peanuts (20g), preserved radish/chai poh (15g), and pickled cucumber slices. The total set weighs approximately 520g and provides ~450 kcal, 18g protein, 25g fat, and 42g carbohydrate. The watery rice gruel has the highest glycemic index of all rice preparations (GI ~72) due to complete starch gelatinisation and minimal retrograded resistant starch, making it a significant glycaemic concern for T2DM patients — a counterintuitive but clinically important counselling point, as many patients assume soft porridge is 'safer' than firm rice. Sodium is very high at ~1,050mg per set (202mg per 100g), with the salted egg half alone contributing ~600mg and chai poh ~225mg. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork) and is dairy-free. Key clinical modifications: replacing the salted egg with a fresh egg saves ~560mg Na; omitting chai poh saves ~225mg Na. Found at hawker centres across Singapore, particularly at older estates, and is strongly associated with elderly Teochew Chinese dietary traditions. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database with component calculation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database via component calculation for the full set. HPB Singapore is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI value of ~72 for watery rice gruel is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for rice congee/porridge. Confidence 1.0 for macros; 0.9 for GI (analogous estimate).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Teochew Muay set (watery rice gruel + braised pork belly + salted egg + peanuts + chai poh) is not available as a single entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central; component-level fatty acid data would require individual ingredient disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite set is not reported as a single value in HPB Singapore Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for this traditional preparation per HPB Singapore data). All core macros (protein_g 3.5g, fat_g 4.8g, carbohydrate_g 8.1g) are non-zero and fully supported. NOT halal — contains pork belly. Dietary cholesterol is high (~65mg per 100g, ~338mg per 520g set) primarily from salted egg yolk and pork belly. GI ~72 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for watery rice congee/gruel; confidence 0.9. GL per set = (72 × 38.1) / 100 ≈ 27.4. Sodium per set ~1,050mg: salted egg half ~600mg + chai poh ~225mg + braising sauces ~225mg. FoodEx2 A0KDA used as closest parent; no exact code for Teochew Muay set exists. FoodOn URI not available for this prepared hawker dish.
